spec_match.rationale: Walks the three canonical DBSC attack-class scenarios (honest, remote replay, local replay) that the webappsec-dbsc explainer uses to motivate hardware-bound keys.

interactive.rationale: Three scenario buttons drive a play/reset stepper that animates the same handshake with different actors — clearly above the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index promises 'pick a scenario… step through the DBSC handshake to see why a hardware-backed key kills stolen-cookie attacks' — page delivers exactly that with the three scenarios.

edge_cases.rationale: Covers honest + two attacker paths. Missing: the registration-failure path (TPM unavailable falls back to long cookie), the refresh-fail-mid-session path, and the cross-device sync attack where the attacker controls a SECOND TPM-backed device.

references.rationale: Links chromestatus.com/feature/5140168270413824. The webappsec-dbsc spec link is present at the feature index.

design_system.rationale: Scenario buttons override background+color together (rule 6); colours read from design-system variables; no static-card.

openQuestions.title: Add a TPM-unavailable / software-fallback scenario
openQuestions.detail: DBSC degrades gracefully when there's no hardware enclave — the session still works but loses the device binding guarantee. That fallback is the most common real-world deployment path (older Linux desktops, headless CI, etc.). Add a fourth scenario button that walks the registration with software keys and contrasts the threat model.

openQuestions.title: Add the cross-device sync attacker class
openQuestions.detail: DBSC defends against malware on a SECOND machine; it does NOT defend against malware on the user's same machine after key access. The 'local replay' scenario hand-waves this; an explicit 'attacker also has TPM access' branch with the failure mode visible would deepen the threat model story.

openQuestions.title: Show the actual JWT signature on a refresh step
openQuestions.detail: The walkthrough animates the steps but never shows the actual JWT payload + signature that the browser sends to the refresh endpoint. A 'show the wire' toggle that pops open the signed JWT (header/claims/sig) would connect the visual story to the protocol the spec defines.

summary: Best of the four for narrative — three real scenarios drive the same handshake animation, the win is visible. Add the software-fallback scenario and a wire-level JWT view to close the gap with the spec's full threat model.
